questions.question: What does the 'D' in the DISASTER Paradigm stand for?
options: A. Decontamination
B. Detection
C. Defibrillation
D. Documentation
questions.rationale: The paradigm begins with Detection — recognizing that an event's needs exceed available resources.

questions.question: Which is the correct order of the DISASTER Paradigm components?
options: A. Triage, Detection, Safety, Command, Support, Hazards, Evacuation, Recovery
B. Detection, Incident command, Safety and security, Assess hazards, Support, Triage and treatment, Evacuation, Recovery
C. Detection, Safety, Triage, Command, Hazards, Support, Recovery, Evacuation
D. Incident command, Detection, Triage, Evacuation, Safety, Support, Hazards, Recovery
questions.rationale: The ordered checklist is Detection → Incident command → Safety and security → Assess hazards → Support → Triage and treatment → Evacuation → Recovery.

questions.question: You are the first trained responder at a building collapse with many casualties. After sizing up the scene, what comes next?
options: A. Begin treating the nearest casualty
B. Establish and announce incident command
C. Start evacuating patients to the closest hospital
D. Wait for a supervisor before doing anything
questions.rationale: After detection, the first trained responder establishes incident command, creating a coordination point; command transfers later as senior officers arrive. Patient care before command and safety leads to chaos.

questions.question: What is the correct safety priority order at a disaster scene?
options: A. Patients, bystanders, team, self
B. Self, team, public/bystanders, patients
C. Team, patients, self, public
D. There is no priority order
questions.rationale: Protect yourself first, then your team, then the public, then patients — an injured rescuer consumes resources instead of providing them.

questions.question: At a bombing scene, hazard assessment must specifically consider:
options: A. Only the original blast site
B. Secondary devices deliberately targeting responders
C. Weather forecasts for next week
D. Hospital bed counts
questions.rationale: Intentional events may include secondary devices timed to strike responders — a core reason hazard assessment is continuous and staging discipline matters.

questions.question: Why should resource support be requested early in an incident?
options: A. To impress command
B. Because resources arrive with a delay — order for the incident you will have, not the one you see
C. Because requests expire
D. It should actually be requested last
questions.rationale: Mutual aid, supplies, and transport all take time to arrive. Anticipatory requests keep resources flowing in phase with the incident's growth.

questions.question: Which transport decision best reflects disaster evacuation principles?
options: A. Send all 40 casualties to the nearest hospital
B. Distribute casualties by severity across multiple appropriate receiving facilities
C. Transport only the walking wounded
D. Hold all patients on scene until every one is fully stabilized
questions.rationale: Dumping every casualty on the closest hospital recreates the disaster inside it. Command distributes patients by acuity and capability across the system.

questions.question: Clusters of patients with similar unusual symptoms appearing at several EDs across a city over two days most likely represents:
options: A. Coincidence requiring no action
B. The detection phase of an insidious (possibly biological/chemical) event
C. A staffing problem
D. Normal seasonal variation always
questions.rationale: Covert releases and epidemics announce themselves as syndromic clusters. Recognizing them as a detection signal — and notifying public health — triggers the response system.

questions.question: A physician bypasses the incident command post and begins directing ambulances personally. This behavior is:
options: A. Ideal physician leadership
B. Freelancing — it breaks unified coordination and accountability
C. Required by medical ethics
D. The definition of unified command
questions.rationale: Acting outside the command structure (freelancing) fragments the operation, duplicates or contradicts orders, and endangers the responder. Clinical skills are contributed inside assigned roles.

questions.question: When should recovery activities begin?
options: A. Only after the scene is fully closed
B. During the response itself
C. One year after the event
D. Never — recovery is not a responder concern
questions.rationale: Recovery starts during response: restoring systems, tracking patients, supporting responders and survivors, and documenting lessons learned.

questions.question: The first radio report from the initial responder at an MCI should communicate:
options: A. A complete list of patient names
B. Estimated casualty count, hazards, resources needed, and that command is established
C. Only the responder's unit number
D. The names of the deceased
questions.rationale: The early size-up report paints the detection picture and mobilizes the system: casualties, hazards, needs, and command status.

questions.question: Which action violates the Safety and security component?
options: A. Staging until the scene is declared safe
B. Entering an unstable collapsed structure alone to reach a casualty faster
C. Wearing appropriate PPE
D. Establishing a perimeter
questions.rationale: Entering an uncleared, unstable structure alone risks converting a rescuer into a casualty — the exact failure the safety hierarchy exists to prevent.

questions.question: In the DISASTER Paradigm, triage and treatment are performed using:
options: A. First-come, first-served order
B. A standardized method (SALT) serving the greatest good for the greatest number
C. Alphabetical order
D. Insurance status
questions.rationale: BDLS teaches SALT, the CDC-sponsored national standard, to sort casualties so limited resources produce the most total benefit.

questions.question: Why is hazard assessment described as continuous rather than one-time?
options: A. To create paperwork
B. Scenes evolve — collapse, fire spread, aftershocks, or secondary devices can appear after arrival
C. Because the first assessment is always wrong
D. It is actually one-time
questions.rationale: The hazard picture changes: what was safe on arrival may become lethal. Continuous reassessment protects responders throughout the operation.

questions.question: Which pairing of paradigm component and action is correct?
options: A. Support — establishing a perimeter
B. Evacuation — distributing patients to appropriate facilities
C. Detection — providing definitive care
D. Recovery — initial scene size-up
questions.rationale: Evacuation covers moving the right patient to the right destination. Perimeters are safety/security; size-up is detection; definitive care follows triage/treatment.

questions.question: The DISASTER Paradigm applies to which events?
options: A. Only explosions
B. Only natural disasters
C. All hazards — floods, bombings, epidemics, chemical and radiological events alike
D. Only declared federal disasters
questions.rationale: The paradigm is BDLS's all-hazards checklist: the same ordered priorities regardless of the event's cause.
